reasoning: Every leg of the claim verifies against the code. (1) Commit b097efb changed handleSpeak (src/App.tsx:239-251) from 'speakText unconditionally + ws.speak when connected' to 'ws.speak if ws.isConnected else speakText'. isConnected is raw socket state (useWebSocket.tsx:486-503) with no TTS-health input. (2) Backend: pyttsx3 import failure (main.py:64-69) leaves the WS server fully operational; TTSEngine.__init__ returns early (672-673) with _queue=None/engine=None, so speak() falls to the silent 'TTS not available. Would speak:' no-op (737-739). pyttsx3.init() exceptions set engine=None (703-705) and the worker loop discards utterances with only a log line (713-719). A runAndWait hang (known pyttsx3 failure mode) blocks the worker at line 717 with no watchdog, silently swallowing all future speech; the cross-thread engine.stop() in stop() (750-764, against an engine with documented thread affinity per line 687) is wired to routine patient STOP buttons (WebBrowsingScreen.tsx:1242, 2521), making the hang reachable through normal interaction. (3) No error surfacing: the 'speak' handler (main.py:1860 -> _speak at 2560-2566) sends no ack or failure message; the 'connected' handshake (3234-3238) carries no TTS field; grep confirms no TTS-status message exists anywhere in the protocol or frontend hook. (4) The persistent emergency button (App.tsx:112) routes through the same handleSpeak, and speechSynthesis exists nowhere else in src/, so the regression silences emergency speech too. Pre-change, browser synthesis fired on every utterance, so a dead backend TTS still left a working voice â€” the diff removed that redundancy. wouldManifest is true with one qualifier: it requires a TTS fault concurrent with a healthy socket, so it does not occur on a healthy install; but the deterministic paths (missing/failed pyttsx3, SAPI5 init failure) and the probabilistic path (runAndWait hang, plausibly triggered by routine cross-thread STOP presses mid-utterance under the just-enabled async worker mode) are realistic over the device's lifetime, and when hit the patient is completely mute â€” emergency included â€” with a green connected indicator and no recovery short of restarting the backend.
suggestedFix: Make the fallback condition reflect TTS health, not socket health: (a) include tts_available (TTS_AVAILABLE and engine is not None after init) in the 'connected' handshake (main.py:3234) and push a tts_status update if the engine dies; (b) have _speak (main.py:2560) reply with a speak_result {ok} message â€” ok=false whenever engine is None or the queue depth indicates a stalled worker; (c) in handleSpeak (App.tsx:239), use the backend only when isConnected AND ttsAvailable, otherwise speakText; additionally arm a one-shot ~1.5s watchdog after ws.speak that triggers speakText if no speak_result ok arrives (preserving the single-voice fix since the backend produced no audio). On the backend, route stop() through the worker queue instead of calling engine.stop() cross-thread, and add a worker watchdog that re-inits the engine and flags tts_status if runAndWait exceeds a sane utterance timeout.
